title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 4 — Emperor Shao Deposed: Prince of Chenliu Becomes Emperor / Schemes against Dong Zhuo: Mengde Presents a Sword
text:
Let us turn now whenDong Zhuowas intent on slayingYuan Shao.Li Ruchecked him, saying, “Our undertaking has not been consolidated yet. Do not kill indiscriminately!”

Yuan Shao, with his hand still on his treasured sword, took his leave of the other ministers and departed from the assembly. He hung the symbols of his office at the eastern gate and left forJi province.

Dong Zhuothen said to theGrand Tutor,Yuan Wei, “Your nephew was quite disrespectful, yet seeing your face here, I forgave him. But what say you on the matter of succession?”

Yuan Weisaid, “Your view is right,Grand Commandant.”

Dong Zhuosaid, “Anyone who dares obstruct this great scheme will be punished according to military law!”

The ministers, trembling with fear, all told him, “We will obey your command.” Once the feast had ended,Dong Zhuoasked one of thePalace Attendants,Zhou Bi, and one of theColonels,Wu Qiong, “What say you onYuan Shaoleaving in such amanner?”1•••

Zhou Bi’s father had been in a war withDong Zhuo, both men were already at the capital whenDong Zhuoarrived and advocated a policy of sending talent to the provinces.↩

Yuan Shaostands up toDong Zhuobut the court, including old guard like his uncle, are cowed by the military man.

Zhou Bitold him, “Yuan Shaoleft in a state of great anger, and if you press him too hard, he is sure to attempt something untoward. Furthermore, the Yuan clan have been sowing favors for four generations, with their clients and former subordinates spread across the realm. If they were to enlist the aid of the gentry to muster forces, heroes and stalwarts would rise up in kind, and you would lose control of everything east of the mountains. You had better pardon him and appoint him as theadministratorof some commandery. He will be so glad to have escaped conviction that he will surely cause noconcern.”2•••

The Yuan clan had been a very powerful family throughout the Later Han, assuming the highest of ranks in the imperial court for four generations. They were also the wealthiest family in the capital. In a society where patron and client relations being a threat to central authority, the Yuan clan had generations of clients, connections and favors behind them.↩

Wu Qiongadded, “Yuan Shaois fond of scheming, but he is indecisive. He is not to be feared. It really would be best to give him a commandery and thus win popular favor.”

Dong Zhuoaccepted this advice and that same day dispatched a messenger to appointYuan ShaoasGrand AdministratorofBohai.3•••

Bohai was out of the Yuan traditional power-base,Yuan Shaowas now in the northern province of Ji, away from the court and the problem for the head of Ji to deal with.↩

On thefirst day of the ninth month,the Emperorwas invited to ascend theHall of Resplendent Virtuebefore a great assembly of civil and military officials.Dong Zhuodrew out his sword, faced the gathering, and said, “TheSon of Heavenis a weakling, unworthy of ruling this land. I have here a certain document, which will be read aloud.”

And he badeLi Ruto read it out loud:

Emperor Ling the Filialtoo soon left his people. The Emperor is the one to whom all people look. Upon thepresent EmperorHeaven has conferred but small gifts: in dignity and deportment he is deficient and in mourning he is remiss. None of his virtues have revealed themselves, and he brings disgrace to the imperial title.

As for theEmpress Dowager, she has educated him without the bearing of a mother, and in presiding over the state, she has brought about confusion and disorder. There is much speculation over the sudden death ofEmpress Dowager Dong. How can we permit such lapses in the bonds of human relations or the ties of Heaven and Earth?

Conversely, thePrinceofChenliu,Liu Xie, is sage and virtuous, possessing a handsome appearance and solemn propriety. In the observance of mourningHis Late Majesty, he never uttered a wayward word. All the realm has heard his praises sung. He ought to inherit the grand design and continue the legacy of ten thousandgenerations.•••

The most positive review of Emperor Ling one might ever see…

The Emperoris hereby deposed and created asPrinceofHongnong, and the Empress Dowager shall relinquish theadministration.4•••

Established protocol for deposing a young (but soon to be inconveniently of age) Emperor is have the Dowager claim a failure to mourn properly and showing lack of virtue. Less established is doing so five minutes after arriving in the capital and publicly removing the Dowager from her position in the process.↩

Let us acclaim thePrinceofChenliuas Emperor, heeding Heaven and obeying the people, in order to reassure the wishes of both the living and the dead.

OnceLi Ruhad finished reading,Dong Zhuosharply ordered his attendants to leadthe Emperordown from the throne, remove his seal and ribbon of office, and cause him to kneel facing north like a subject, acknowledging submission and declaring his intention to obey. Moreover, he bade Empress Dowager He strip off her dress of ceremony and await the imperial command.

Both victims of this oppression wept, and not a minister present failed to be deeply affected. From the foot of the stairs, one of them vented his indignation, crying out, “The traitorous ministerDong Zhuodares to make a mockery of Heaven’s arrangements! May the blood of my neck tarnish him!” And with this he rushed atDong Zhuoto strike him with hisivory tablet of office.5•••

Anivory tablet of office, some three feet in length by about three inches wide, was carried by officials of a certain rank in his hands when at court and used for writing.↩

Dong Zhuo, in a rage, called upon his guards to subdue his attacker: it was one of theSecretariat Minister,Ding Guan.Dong ZhuoorderedDing Guandragged out and beheaded.Ding Guannever stopped cursingDong Zhuo, and neither his spirit nor expression faltered even unto the moment of his death.

A poem in later times lamented:

The traitor Dong concealed his plot with guile,

To usurp the throne,Han‘s lineage defile.

With folded arms the courtiers stood, save one

Lord Ding, who dared to cry that wrong was done.

Dong Zhuothen invited thePrinceofChenliuto take the throne. Once the ministers had finished congratulating thenew Emperor,Dong Zhuoordered that Empress Dowager He, thePrinceofHongnong, and theimperial consortLady Tang be removed to thePalace of Perpetual Calm, where the palace gates were to be locked and no minister was to visit them without permission.

The pitiableyoung Emperorwas thus enthroned in the fourth month only to be deposed by the ninth. As for the Prince of Chenliu whomDong Zhuohad just enthroned, thisLiu Xie, styledBohe, was the second son of the late Emperor Ling and the same man who would become known to history asEmperor Xian. At this time he was but nine years of age, five years younger than his brother. A new reign period,Chuping or Inceptive Peace, wasproclaimed.•••

I wonder what possibly drew the controller of the government to the prince with years left till he was old enough to rule.

Dong Zhuowas now appointed asChancellor6and granted several other honors: he was referred to by his title rather than his name,7he was not obliged to hurry to his assigned place when entering court, and permitted to keep his sword and shoes on within the palace.8No one else had such power and prestige.Li Ruurged him constantly to employ men of reputation so that he might capture public esteem, and he recommendedCai Yongon account of his talent.9Yet whenDong Zhuosummoned him,Cai Yongwould not go. Incensed,Dong Zhuosent an agent to tellCai Yong, “If you do not come, your whole clan will be exterminated.”Cai Yong, frightened, gave in and appeared.Dong Zhuowas delighted to see him, and promoted him thrice in a month; he even appointed him as aPalace Attendantand showed him exceptional closeness andgenerosity.•••

The Later Han had power split among the Three Excellencies, the revival of the long dead rank of Chancellor putDong Zhuofirmly at the head of government.↩

He was referred to his title in the sense that in court, the ushers announced his arrival only by declaring his title without uttering his name.↩

Powerful officials could sometimes be given formal exemptions from such etiquette but could leave doubts about loyalty↩

Cai Yongwas mentioned in the first chapter, warning about bad omens. As the star scholar of his day, his joining the court was a coup.↩

Meanwhile theyoung emperor,Empress Dowager He, and Lady Tang were imprisoned inYong’an palaceand found their stipends of clothing and food gradually diminishing. Theyoung emperorwept incessantly. One day a pair of swallows gliding to and fro in the courtyard moved him toverse:10•••

Yong’an was a palace within the capital.↩

Fresh vernal grasses tint the dense fog;

graceful and gentle do the swallows soar.

The Luo river, a stretch of blue,

sparks admiration and envy from wayfarers!

But deep beyond the azure clouds

are my former palaces and halls.

Who would be so loyal and honorable

to ease the grievance in my heart?

A spy, tasked byDong Zhuoto keep thedeposed Emperorunder regular surveillance, took record of this poem and showed it to his master.

Dong Zhuosaid, “So he shows his resentment by writing poems, eh? A good excuse to kill them all!”

Li Ruwas sent with ten warriors into the palace to kill theEmperor. TheEmperor, the Empress Dowager, and the imperial concubine were atop a tower when the maid announced the arrival ofLi Ru. TheEmperorwas terrified.

PresentlyLi Ruentered and offered a cup of poisoned wine to the formerEmperor, who asked him what the occasion was.

“As spring is the season of blending and harmonious interchange,ChancellorDongsends a special cup of the wine of longevity,” said he.

“If it be the wine of longevity, may the first sip be yours,” said Empress Dowager He.

“So you won’t drink it, eh?”Li Rusaid in a huff. He called forward attendants bearing daggers and white cords, saying, “If you won’t drink the wine, you can try one of these instead!”

Then Lady Tang knelt and said, “Oh sir, let this handmaiden drink in place of the Emperor if only you will spare the lives of mother and child.”

“And who are you, to die for a prince?” mockedLi Ru.

Then he thrust the cup towardsEmpress Dowager Heand said, “May the first sip be yours!”

She only swore at the short-sightedness of her brotherHe Jin, who had invited such traitors into the capital and thus brought about this disaster.

Next,Li Rupressured the Emperor.

“Let me say farewell to my mother,” begged he, and with great emotion, he sang:

The heaven and earth are changed alas!

And the sun and the moon leave their courses.

Once I had an empire, and now have naught but a fief!

Oppressed by an arrogant minister, my life nears its end;

everything fails me and vain are my falling tears.

Lady Tang sang as well:

August Heaven is to be rent asunder, Mother Earth to fall away.

I, handmaid of an Emperor, would grieve if I followed him not.

We have come to the parting of ways, the living and the dead walk not together.

Alas! Must I be left alone so early? How my heartgrieves!•••

Both these songs, as they face their fate, are based on their historical poems

Having sung , they fell into the arms of each other, weeping.

“TheChancelloris awaiting my report,” complainedLi Ru, “and you delay too long. Hoping someone might save you?”

“Dong Zhuo, ye traitor who hounds a woman and child to death! Almighty Heaven shall lend thee no aid!” cursed Empress Dowager He. “And you, who aid him in his wickedness! May all your clans be exterminated!”

This so infuriatedLi Ruthat he laid his own hands upon her and threw her from the tower. Then he bade the warriors strangle Lady Tang with a cord and force the poisoned wine down the throat of the young emperor.

He returned and reported the achievement of the cruel deed to his master, who bade them buried outside of the city. From then on,Dong Zhuowent into the palace every night, having his way with the palace women and sleeping on the imperial couch.

On one occasion,Dong Zhuoled his soldiers out of the city and marched toYangcheng. It was the second month by then, and the local villagers, men and women, were assembled from all sides for the annual festival.Dong Zhuo’s troops surrounded the place and slaughtered all the men, while seizing the women and all the wealth, binding them to carts to bring back. They hung over a thousand heads from their carts as they returned to the capital, spreading word that they were just back from a great victory over some rebels.Dong Zhuohad the heads burned beneath the walls and distributed the women and treasures out among his soldiers.

TheColonel of Charging Cavalry,Wu Fu, styledDeyu, was disgusted and unnerved at the sight ofDong Zhuo’s depravity. He regularly wore a breastplate underneath his court dress and concealed a sharp dagger, hoping to find a chance to killDong Zhuo. One day, whenDong Zhuocame to court,Wu Fumet him on the steps,Wu Fupulled out his knife to stab at him. YetDong Zhuowas a powerful man and he heldWu Fubetween both hands untilLü Buquickly came to his help and forcedWu Futo the ground.

Dong Zhuoasked, “Who told you to rebel?”

Wu Fuglared at him and cried out, “You are not my prince, I am not your minister; what rebellion is there? Your crimes and vices fill the heavens, and every man would slay you! I regret I cannot tear you asunder with chariots to appease theworld!”•••

It seems as thoughWu Fuis insinuating there is no rebellion becauseDong Zhuowas never his lord to begin with.

Enraged,Dong Zhuobade them take him out and hack him to pieces.Wu Fuonly ceased railing as he ceased to live.

A certain poet in later ages would write a poem in praise ofWu Fu:

Speak ofWu Fuas a loyal Han minister during its end;

his valor was high as the heavens, in all ages unequaled;

He struck a traitor in court, and his fame still persists;

throughout all of time will men call him a hero.

If you must tell of loyalty,

Tell ofWu Fu’s to the Han.

His courage mounted to the skies

When down below was none.

He struck atDong Zhuoin the court;

His fame is with us still.

Forever and ever he’s won the name

Of a man of iron will.

ThereafterDong Zhuoalways went to and from court well guarded.

AtBohai,Yuan Shaoheard ofDong Zhuo’s misuse of power and sent an agent to bring a secret letter toWang Yun. The letter went something like this:

“The rebelDong Zhuomocks Heaven and has deposed his liege; men cannot bear to speak of such actions! Yet you suffer his aggressions as if you knew naught of them. Is this any way for a subject of the state to show his gratitude or prove his loyalty? Though I am assembling troops and training soldiers, wishing to sweep clean the sovereign’s domain, I dare not make any rash movement. If you are willing, then find an opportunity to plot against this man; call upon me to drive him out and I shall be at your command.”

Yet althoughWang Yunreceived the letter, his pondering could devise no suitable scheme. Then one day, when he noticed that the whole crowd of senior ministers had gathered near the court chambers, he said to his colleagues, “As today is this old fellow’s humble birthday, I pray you shall all condescend to come to a little party in my meager abode this evening.”

The assembled officials all said, “We shall come and wish you long life.”

That night, the tables were spread in a back room and the chief ministers gathered there. Yet once the wine had made a few rounds,Wang Yunsuddenly covered his face and began to weep.

The assembled officials were shocked and asked, “Your Excellency, what brings out such grief on your esteemedbirthday?”•••

Clearly hadn’t heard the saying, “It’s my party, and I’ll cry if I want to.”

“Today is not my birthday,” replied he. “I only wished to call you all together and I fearedDong Zhuowould be suspicious, thus I made that the excuse. He insults the Emperor and does as he wishes, and the altars of the state itself are in imminent peril. I think of the days when our illustrious founder Emperor Gao destroyedQin, annihilatedChu, and obtained the realm. Who could have foreseen that his legacy which has passed down all this time should now perish at the hands ofDong Zhuo? That is why I weep.”

Most of the assembled ministers soon burst into tears as well.

But among those seated, there was one man who clapped his hands and laughed. He said, “If all the court ministers weep from dusk until dawn and from dawn until dusk, will their tears slayDong Zhuo?”

Wang Yunlooked to see who it was. It was theColonel of Agile Cavalry,Cao Cao.

Wang Yunseethed, “Your own ancestors received the bounty of the Han court as well. And yet, instead of thinking how to repay the state, you laugh?”

Cao Caosaid, “What I am laughing at is nothing more than the incompetence of an assembly like this being unable to come up with a single plan to killDong Zhuo. Talentless I am, I still stand ready to cut off his head at once and to hang it up at the capital gate to appease the realm.”

Wang Yunleft his seat and asked him, “Mengde, how can you be sosure?”11•••

MengdeisCao Cao’s style name, a sign of respect fromWang Yunto a more junior figure.↩

“I’ve only been humbling myself to serveDong Zhuo,”Cao Caocontinued, “so that I can find a chance to destroy him. Now that he’s begun to trust me, I can take an opportunity to approach him. I’ve heard you have a precious ‘Seven Star Blade’; if you will lend it to me so I can go into theChancellor’s office and kill him, even if I die I won’t regret it!”

Wang Yunsaid, “So your heart is true after all. How blessed the realm is!”

With this he himself poured out a goblet and offered it toCao Cao, who drank deep and swore an oath. After this, the precious sword was brought out and given toCao Cao, who concealed it. As soon as he had finished his wine, he got up and took leave of the ministers as he left the hall. Before long the others dispersed aswell.•••

Wang Yunis a senior court minister who is witnessing the court being desecrated by a tyrant. But he requires prodding fromYuan Shaoto even consider acting. He and other senior court officials can think of nothing to save the Dynasty. In stark contrast,Cao Cao, a younger man and a mere colonel, mocks them as ineffectual and has waited patiently until he came up with a plan, intending on driving it forward himself.

The next day, with thebladegirded on,Cao Caocame to theChancellor’s office.

“Where is theChancellor?” asked he.

“In the small guest room,” answered the attendants.

Cao Caowent in and found his host seated on a couch, withLü Bustanding in attendance to the side.

Dong Zhuosaid, “Why have you come so late,Mengde?”

Cao Caosaid, “My horse is out of condition and slow.”

Dong Zhuoturned toLü Buand said, “We have some fine horses sent fromXiliang.Fengxian, do go out and pick out a good one as a present forMengde.”Lü Buleft asinstructed.12•••

Xiliang was known for its good horses↩

“The traitor deserves to die!” thoughtCao Cao, and he considered pulling outthe bladeand stabbingDong Zhuothen and there, yet fearingDong Zhuo’s great strength, he resisted the impulse to act prematurely.

Dong Zhuowas so fat that he could not remain sitting upright for long, so soon he stretched himself out and rolled over on the couch, facing the rear wall. “This tyrant is finished!” thought the assassin, and he swiftly pulled outthe precious blade. Just as he was about to strike,Dong Zhuohappened to glance at a metal reflector on his clothing and saw the reflection ofCao Caobehind him witha bladein hand.

He turned around suddenly and asked, “What are you doing,Mengde?” And at that moment,Lü Bureturned with the requested horse.

Panicking,Cao Caodropped on his knees while holding out his hands to offerthe blade, and said, “I have a precious blade here, which I wish to present totheChancelloras a token of mygratitude.”•••

This can be interpreted in many ways. Some people believe he improvised in a state of panic, but others believe that he requested a bejeweled sword so that he could present it as a gift in caseDong Zhuowoke up as a back-up plan. In terms of personality, this event shows either his quick wit or his lack of honor, or perhaps even both.

Dong Zhuotook thebladeand looked it over. It was over a chi in length, inlaid with seven precious stones, and very keen; a fine blade indeed. He gestured forLü Buto take it, whileCao Caoremoved the sheath and handed it toLü Buaswell.13•••

A chi is generally over 24 centimeters or 9.45 inches↩

Dong ZhuobroughtCao Caoout into the courtyard to look at the horse.Cao Caooffered his thanks and said, “Let me test it by giving it a ride.”Dong Zhuobade the attendants to bring saddle and bridle.Cao Caoled the creature outside the office, leaped into the saddle, laid on his whip vigorously, and galloped away to the southeast.

Lü Buremarked toDong Zhuo, “Just as I was coming up, it seemed to me as ifCao Caowas going to stab you, but that he was caught off guard by your shout and presented theweaponinstead.”

“I suspected him too,” saidDong Zhuo. Just as they were talking about it,Li Ruchanced to appear, andDong Zhuotold him about what had happened.

“Cao Caohas no family here at the capital, but only lives in a temporary lodging,”Li Ruadvised them. “Send for him. If he comes without any resistance, then thebladereally was meant as a gift, but if he makes any excuses, then he was surely trying to assassinate you, and you can promptly arrest and question him.”

Dong Zhuoagreed, and sent four prison warders to look forCao Cao. After some time, they came back and reported, “Cao Caodid not return to his lodging, but rode in hot haste out of the eastern gate, replying to the gate-warden’s questions by saying that he was ‘on urgent public business for theChancellor’. He has since gone off at full speed.”

“The villain was up to no good and now he has scurried away; there is no doubt that he meant assassination,” saidLi Ru.

Dong Zhuo, in a fit of fury, said, “I treated him so well, yet he sought to kill me!”

Li Rusaid, “Others must have conspired with him. When we catch him, we shall know all about it.”

And soDong Zhuobade that letters and pictures ofCao Caobe sent everywhere so as to catch him. Whoever turned him in was to be rewarded with a thousand gold and a marquisate of ten thousand households, while anyone who sheltered him would be convicted of the samecrime.14•••

A marquisate would give them noble status and the tax income of ten thousand households each year, highly desirable considering the chaos the empire faced recently.↩

Having scurried out of the capital,Cao Caofled towards his home ofQiao commandery. Yet while passing throughZhongmou county, he was apprehended by the guards watching one of the passes, who brought him to see thePrefect.Cao Caosaid, “I am a merchant, surnamed Huangfu.” ThePrefectscanned his face most closely and remained in deep thought.

He then said, “When I was seeking a post at the capital, I knew you asCao Cao. Why do you try to conceal your identity? You are under arrest. Tomorrow, I will deliver you to the capital for the reward.”

He gave the soldiers wine and food as a reward and sent them away.

About midnight, thePrefectsent a trusted servant to secretly bringCao Caointo the courtyard for interrogation.

He asked, “I heard that theChancellorwas not unkind to you. Why did you try to harm him?”

Cao Caosaid, “How can swallows and sparrows comprehend the ambition of cranes? You have now caught me. Return me so that you can claim your reward.”

ThePrefectsent away his attendants and addressedCao Cao, “Do not despise me. I am no mere hireling, I just have not yet found my true lord.”

SaidCao Cao, “My ancestors enjoyed the bounty of theHanfor generations; would I be any different from some bird or beast if I did not desire to repay it? I only deigned to serveDong Zhuoso that I could find an opportunity against him and remove this evil from the empire. But I have failed for this time. Such is the will of Heaven!”•••

The concept of men’s fates being bound by the heavens will be a running theme.

ThePrefectasked, “In that case, where are you headed?”

Cao Caosaid, “I am returning to my hometown, where I will send out a forged edict summoning all the landed lords across the realm to raise troops and executeDong Zhuotogether. This is my intention!”

Upon hearing this, thePrefectpersonally loosened the bonds of his prisoner, led him to the upper seat, and bowed twice, saying, “You are truly a loyal and righteous gentleman of the realm!”Cao Caoreturned the bow and asked thePrefectabout his surname and forename. ThePrefectsaid, “I amChen Gong, styledGongtai. My aged mother and my wife and children are all inDong commandery. I am so moved by your loyalty and justice that I will abandon this one office and follow you into hiding.”

Cao Caowas very delighted. That night,Chen Gongcollected some money for the expenses of their journey and gaveCao Caoa different garment. Then each of them took a sword and rode away towardCao Cao’s hometown. Three days later, they had just reachedChenggaoas the sun was getting low.Cao Caopointed with his whip to a hamlet deep in the woods and toldChen Gong, “There lives my uncle,Lü Boshe, a sworn-brother of my father. Suppose we go and ask news of my family and seek shelter for the night?”

Chen Gongsaid, “Very well!” And the two people arrived before the farm, dismounted, and entered to seeLü Boshe.

Lü Boshesaid, “I heard that the imperial court has sent orders everywhere, urgently calling for your arrest. Your father has already leftChenliuto go into hiding as well. How did this all happen?”

Cao Caotold him what had happened, saying, “Had it not been forPrefectChen, I would have already been hacked to pieces.”

Lü Boshebowed low toChen Gongand said, “Had my worthy nephew not met you, good sir, the Cao family would have been extinguished! Be at ease and rest, for the hour is late. You may get some sleep in my humble cottage.”

After speaking, he went into the inner chamber where he stayed a long time. When he came out, he said toChen Gong, “There is no good wine in the house. Let me go out to the west village to treat you both.”

After saying that, he hastily mounted his donkey and rode away. The two travelers sat for a long time. Suddenly, they heard the sound of knives being sharpened at the back of the house.

Cao Caosaid, “Lü Bosheis not my real uncle, and I am beginning to doubt the meaning of his departure. Let uslisten.”15•••

Cao Caois saying thatLü Bosheis not related to him by blood.↩

The two men silently stepped out into a straw hut at the back. Presently, someone said, “Bind before killing, eh?”

Cao Caosaid, “As I thought! If we do not strike first, we will be captured!”

Thus they drew their swords and dashed in. They asked no questions and slew the whole household, male and female. Eight lives were lost in that moment.

When they came to the kitchen, they found a pig, bound and ready to be killed.

Chen Gongsaid, “You were overthinking,Mengde! You erred and killed honest folk!”

They at once mounted their horses and rode away. Yet they had not traveled twolibefore they metLü Boshewhipping his donkey forward, dangling two vessels of wine from the saddle and holding fruit and vegetables in his hand.

He called out, “Worthy nephew and good sir, why are you hurrying off?”

Cao Caosaid, “Accused people dare not linger.”

Lü Boshesaid, “I already had my family butcher a pig. Worthy nephew and good sir, can you spare just one night? Please return quickly!”

ButCao Caodid not look back, urging his horse forward. When he was just a few paces away fromLü Boshe, he suddenly drew his sword and turned his horse.

“Who is that coming along?” he called towardsLü Boshe.

WhenLü Bosheturned his head to look, at the same instantCao Caoswung his sword and cut him down from the donkey.

Chen Gongwas astonished.

He said, “Moments ago, you merely erred, but what is this now?”

Cao Caosaid, “WhenLü Boshegot home and saw so much of his family had been killed, would he have been willing to overlook it? If he had brought troops after us, we would have perished.”

Chen Gongsaid, “To kill deliberately is very wicked!”

Cao Caosaid, “I would rather betray the world than let the world betrayme!”•••

This has become one of the most iconic lines ofCao Caothat helps (for positive and negative reasons) perceptions of him

Chen Gongremained silent. They rode some distance overnight by moonlight and presently knocked at an inn for shelter. After feeding their horses,Cao Caowas soon asleep.Chen Gongthought:

“I regardedCao Caoto be a good man, so I left my office to follow him. Yet, he is just a lout with the heart of a wolf. If I spare him now, he will eventually cause more trouble.”

He thought to draw his sword and killCao Cao. And so it was:

His heart was filled with venom towards wicked men;

forCao CaoandDong Zhuohave turned out to be akin men.

The further fortunes ofCao Caowill be told in the next chapter.
